text: During laparoscopy, the surgeon confirms the presence of a large mass at the splenic flexure. The mass shows no evidence of infiltration into the ligament of Treitz, allowing for consideration of a multivisceral resection.
The surgeon begins the procedure by opening the mesentery at the splenic flexure. The left colic artery is identified, isolated, clipped, and divided.
The surgeon identifies the inferior mesenteric vein near the tail of the pancreas, clips it, and divides it using surgical instruments.
The surgeon lifts and detaches the mesentery of the splenic flexure from the left kidney, removing the genital fascia for oncological purposes.
The surgeon divides the mesentery of the transverse colon up to the colonic wall. The gastrocolic ligament is then divided, and the omentum is transacted distal to the tumor.
The surgeon divides the transverse colon using a linear stapler, visibly separating the colon into two sections.
The surgeon identifies and isolates the splenic vein and artery, which are visibly infiltrated by the pancreas. These vessels are then clipped and divided as part of the procedure.
The surgeon transects the tail of the pancreas using a linear stapler following the division of the splenic vessels.
The surgeon mobilizes the descending colon and divides it using a linear stapler, ensuring the division occurs distal from the tumor.
The surgeon divides the spleen from its ligament, completing the en bloc resection of the splenic organ.
Following the mobilization of the spleen, the surgeon reveals the infiltration of the greater curvature of the stomach, indicating the extent of the tumor involvement.
The surgeon transects the gastropuploic vessels and divides the greater curvature of the stomach using a linear stapler, completing the resection of these structures.
The surgeon divides the short gastric vessels, completing the resection.

The surgeon performs a solar pubic fanless incision and inserts a wound protector. The specimen is removed using an endo bag, providing double protection to prevent spillage of cancer cells into the abdominal cavity.

Following the removal of the specimen, the surgeon controls choroid hemostasis. Fluorescence angiography is used to assess choroid perfusion in the transverse and descending colon. After confirming adequate perfusion, a side-to-side colonic intracorporeal anastomosis is performed.
